title: Code of Conduct Lesson #3 — Youth Soccer Referee Abuse (Level 3)
source: Based on U.S. Soccer Policy 531-9, effective March 2025

questions.question: What makes Level 3 Abuse fundamentally different from Levels 1 and 2?

answers.label: A
answers.text: It only applies to registered coaches, not spectators

answers.label: B
answers.text: It is no longer just a soccer matter — it can result in criminal charges, civil lawsuits, and permanent lifetime bans

answers.label: C
answers.text: It requires a formal hearing before any penalty is issued

answers.label: D
answers.text: It only applies when physical contact causes a visible injury

questions.question: What is the minimum suspension for Non-Physical Level 3 Abuse (threats and derogatory behavior)?

answers.label: A
answers.text: 4 games

answers.label: B
answers.text: 6 games plus 6–24 months ban

answers.label: C
answers.text: 10 games plus a 12-month ban

answers.label: D
answers.text: Immediate lifetime ban

questions.question: What is the minimum suspension for Physical Level 3 Abuse (violent contact or assault)?

answers.label: A
answers.text: 6 games plus 6–24 months

answers.label: B
answers.text: 10 games plus a 24-month ban

answers.label: C
answers.text: 12 games, up to a lifetime ban

answers.label: D
answers.text: 8 games plus a 12-month ban

questions.question: Saying 'I'll see you in the parking lot after this game' to a referee is classified as what?

answers.label: A
answers.text: Level 2 Non-Physical Abuse — Retaliation Threat

answers.label: B
answers.text: An acceptable expression of frustration if no action follows

answers.label: C
answers.text: Level 3 Non-Physical Abuse — Threat of Physical Violence

answers.label: D
answers.text: Not covered by Policy 531-9 unless spoken on the field

questions.question: Under Policy 531-9, does physical contact need to occur for a Level 3 threat penalty to apply?

answers.label: A
answers.text: Yes — no penalty is issued unless the threat is acted upon

answers.label: B
answers.text: Only if the threat is witnessed by a match official

answers.label: C
answers.text: No — the threat itself is the violation, regardless of whether any contact occurs

answers.label: D
answers.text: Only if the threat is made in writing or on video

questions.question: Saying 'I'm going to shove that card down your throat!' to a referee is classified as what level of abuse?

answers.label: A
answers.text: Level 1 — Dismissive Language

answers.label: B
answers.text: Level 2 — Personal Attack with Profanity

answers.label: C
answers.text: Level 3 — Threatening to Inflict Physical Harm

answers.label: D
answers.text: Not abuse — hyperbolic expressions are not penalized

questions.question: Does Policy 531-9 cover online attacks and social media harassment of referees?

answers.label: A
answers.text: No — only in-person conduct at or near the match venue is covered

answers.label: B
answers.text: Only if the referee is a minor

answers.label: C
answers.text: Yes — cyberbullying and doxing are explicitly named as Level 3 Non-Physical Abuse

answers.label: D
answers.text: Only if the post includes the referee's personal information

questions.question: What is 'doxing' as described in the context of this policy?

answers.label: A
answers.text: Filming a referee during a match without permission

answers.label: B
answers.text: Publishing someone's personal information (such as home address or phone number) online

answers.label: C
answers.text: Submitting a false misconduct report to the league

answers.label: D
answers.text: Recording a referee's name and number to file a post-game complaint

questions.question: A parent posts a referee's home address on Facebook along with accusations of bias. Under Policy 531-9, this is:

answers.label: A
answers.text: Outside the scope of Policy 531-9 since it occurred off the field

answers.label: B
answers.text: Level 2 Abuse — Retaliation

answers.label: C
answers.text: Level 3 Non-Physical Abuse — Cyberbullying and Doxing

answers.label: D
answers.text: Permissible as long as the information is publicly available

questions.question: Intentionally kicking a referee in the shin — even lightly — is classified as what?

answers.label: A
answers.text: Level 1 Physical Abuse — Minor Touching

answers.label: B
answers.text: Level 2 Physical Abuse — Intentional Confrontation

answers.label: C
answers.text: Level 3 Physical Abuse — Violent Contact

answers.label: D
answers.text: Not covered unless the kick causes visible injury

questions.question: In addition to the soccer penalty, what legal classification may apply to kicking a referee?

answers.label: A
answers.text: A civil infraction, resulting in a fine

answers.label: B
answers.text: Assault under criminal law

answers.label: C
answers.text: Trespassing, since the referee is on an official facility

answers.label: D
answers.text: No legal classification — sports disputes are handled internally

questions.question: What does the course state about punching a referee?

answers.label: A
answers.text: It is Level 2 Abuse if the punch does not make full contact

answers.label: B
answers.text: It is assault under criminal law and the most severe category of physical abuse under Policy 531-9

answers.label: C
answers.text: It is only penalized if the referee sustains an injury requiring medical attention

answers.label: D
answers.text: It carries the same penalty as a level 2 shove if no injury results

questions.question: Why is placing hands on or near a referee's neck treated with exceptional severity under Policy 531-9?

answers.label: A
answers.text: Because the neck is a sensitive area — the policy distinguishes it from other physical contact

answers.label: B
answers.text: Because grabbing the neck is considered a form of Level 2 intimidation

answers.label: C
answers.text: Because strangulation is a felony in most U.S. states, and a lifetime ban is the expected outcome

answers.label: D
answers.text: Because referees are trained to report neck contact specifically

questions.question: Can claiming 'I wasn't choking him — I just grabbed his collar' reduce the penalty for placing hands on a referee's neck?

answers.label: A
answers.text: Yes — stated intent is considered in determining the penalty level

answers.label: B
answers.text: Yes — if witnesses confirm the grip was brief

answers.label: C
answers.text: No — no framing of intent changes what the action is under Policy 531-9

answers.label: D
answers.text: Only if the referee does not pursue criminal charges

questions.question: What distinguishes throwing an object near a referee (Level 2) from throwing an object at a referee (Level 3)?

answers.label: A
answers.text: The size and weight of the object thrown

answers.label: B
answers.text: Whether the object actually makes contact with the referee

answers.label: C
answers.text: The intent to strike — throwing to intimidate is Level 2; throwing to hit is Level 3

answers.label: D
answers.text: The location on the field where the throw occurs

questions.question: A water bottle is thrown in anger and strikes the referee. Under Policy 531-9, the minimum penalty is:

answers.label: A
answers.text: 4 games plus a formal warning

answers.label: B
answers.text: 10 games plus 6–24 months

answers.label: C
answers.text: 12 games, up to a lifetime ban

answers.label: D
answers.text: 6 games plus 6–24 months

questions.question: How does Policy 531-9 treat deliberately kicking a ball at a referee after the whistle?

answers.label: A
answers.text: As Level 1 Abuse — dismissive behavior

answers.label: B
answers.text: As Level 2 Abuse — use of object in a non-striking manner

answers.label: C
answers.text: As Level 3 Abuse — use of object in a striking manner

answers.label: D
answers.text: Not as abuse if the player claims the kick was not directed at anyone

questions.question: When a ball is kicked near a referee after the whistle and the referee is hit, how is intent assessed?

answers.label: A
answers.text: Intent is solely determined by the player's own statement

answers.label: B
answers.text: Intent is determined by officials, witnesses, and video footage — claimed intent rarely overrides context

answers.label: C
answers.text: The benefit of the doubt is always given to the player

answers.label: D
answers.text: Only repeat offenders have their intent scrutinized

questions.question: Beyond a suspension, what financial consequences can a referee physically assaulted at a youth match pursue?

answers.label: A
answers.text: None — soccer matches are voluntary activities that limit liability

answers.label: B
answers.text: Only reimbursement of any equipment damaged during the incident

answers.label: C
answers.text: A civil lawsuit for assault, battery, emotional distress, and potentially punitive damages

answers.label: D
answers.text: Only medical costs if the injury required hospitalization

questions.question: What does 'medical liability' mean in the context of Level 3 Abuse, as described in the course?

answers.label: A
answers.text: The offender must complete a health and wellness course

answers.label: B
answers.text: The offender may be held responsible for all medical costs if the referee is injured, including emergency care, rehabilitation, and lost wages

answers.label: C
answers.text: The league is responsible for covering the referee's medical bills

answers.label: D
answers.text: Medical costs are covered by U.S. Soccer's liability insurance

questions.question: What does a permanent lifetime ban mean in practice under Policy 531-9?

answers.label: A
answers.text: The person cannot coach but may still attend matches as a spectator

answers.label: B
answers.text: The person is banned for 10 years with a possible reinstatement review

answers.label: C
answers.text: No games, no sidelines, no tournaments — not even as a spectator, across all U.S. Soccer-affiliated activity forever

answers.label: D
answers.text: The person is banned from the specific league where the offense occurred

questions.question: What happens to a Level 3 penalty on a second offense?

answers.label: A
answers.text: The standard penalty applies — no automatic increase

answers.label: B
answers.text: The punishment doubles (e.g., 6 games becomes 12; 12 games becomes 24)

answers.label: C
answers.text: An automatic lifetime ban is triggered on the second offense

answers.label: D
answers.text: The offender is referred to national arbitration

questions.question: What does the policy state about reinstatement after a third-offense automatic lifetime ban?

answers.label: A
answers.text: Reinstatement is possible after 5 years with good behavior

answers.label: B
answers.text: Reinstatement may be granted after a formal appeal to U.S. Soccer

answers.label: C
answers.text: No reinstatement pathway is described — it is automatic and unconditional

answers.label: D
answers.text: Reinstatement is at the discretion of the local league president

questions.question: What automatic multiplier applies when a Level 3 offense is committed against a referee who is under 18?

answers.label: A
answers.text: The penalty doubles

answers.label: B
answers.text: The penalty triples

answers.label: C
answers.text: An additional 6-month ban is added

answers.label: D
answers.text: The standard penalty applies — age is not a factor at Level 3

questions.question: The course warns that even saying 'I'll see you later' to a referee as a joke can be treated as a threat. Why?

answers.label: A
answers.text: Because any post-match communication with a referee is prohibited

answers.label: B
answers.text: Because the phrase is specifically listed as a Level 3 example in Policy 531-9

answers.label: C
answers.text: Because what means nothing to the adult can mean everything to a teenage referee who hears it as a threat

answers.label: D
answers.text: Because ambiguous statements are always classified at the highest applicable abuse level
